straight_beat.name: Walt Kieniewicz
straight_beat.column:
The Cubs beat the Rays 6-2 yesterday, and that’s what the box score says. It’s the kind of win that makes you scratch your head, because the Cubs have been this close to a breakthrough all season. They’ve been good enough to win, but not good enough to win consistently. This game, though, felt different. Not because of anything flashy, but because of the way the offense clicked and the pitching staff held serve.  

The bats, for once, didn’t waste their chances. They didn’t just string together hits—they carved out runs. The lineup, which has spent so much time in the weeds,

optimist.name: Beatrice 'Bee' Vittorini
optimist.column:
The Cubs didn’t just beat the Rays yesterday—they reminded us why we keep coming back, even when the standings don’t look so pretty. Six runs, two innings, and a bunch of folks in the stands who knew exactly how to cheer for a team that’s still finding its rhythm. You know, the kind of day where the ivy on Wrigley’s walls seems to sway in time with the crowd, like it’s part of the game itself.  

Let’s talk about Crow-Armstrong. That kid’s got a pitcher’s arm and a hitter’s heart. I’ve seen him glide to that

pessimist.name: Tony Gedeski
pessimist.column:
The Cubs won yesterday. That’s the first thing I’ll say. Six to two over the Rays, a team that’s been a dumpster fire all season. But here’s the thing: they’re 6-6, 2.0 games behind in the NL Central. You spend a billion on the marquee and forget to build a bullpen. Every Cubs summer is the same jazz and the same hangover.  

The win was nice, but it’s the kind of victory that feels like a mirage. You look at the box score, and there’s a decent amount of runs scored, but the pitching
